docs.text: Surjections Image Proofs
docs.title: Index

docs.text: How to do an Image/Range Proof Review If we have a function denoted as \(f\) , defined as \(f: D \to C\) , then \(D\) is the domain, and \(C\) is the codomain. The image of the function is \[ f(D) = \{f(x) \mid x \in D\} \] (the set of all outputs of the function for every input in the domain). Example If we have a function: \[ f: \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \to \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}, \quad f(x) = x^2 \] We describe the domain and the codomain as the following sets: Domain ( \(D\) ): \[ \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \] Codomain: \[ \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \] The range (or image) of the function is: \[ f(D) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in D \} \] Since \[ D = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}, \] we can evaluate: \[ f(\{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \} \] \[ = \{ x^2 \mid x \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \} \] \[ = \{(-2)^2, (-1)^2, 0^2, 1^2, 2^2\} \] \[ = \{4, 1, 0\} \] Note: "range" and "image" mean the same thing. You should also notice with this example that even though the image is a subset of the codomain, it may not always be equal to it. Proof Strategies Suppose we have a function \[ f: A \to B \] and we want to prove its range (or image) is \(C\) (with \(A\) , \(B\) , and \(C\) being arbitrary sets). Essentially, we want to show: \[ f(A) = C \] To prove two sets are equal, we need double subset inclusion, i.e., prove both: \[ f(A) \subseteq C \] and \[ C \subseteq f(A). \] Step 1: Show \(f(A) \subseteq C\) This means: \[ \forall y, \quad y \in f(A) \implies y \in C \] Usually, this is just a simple inequality check or "definition unwinding" if \(C\) is an interval of real numbers. Step 2: Show \(C \subseteq f(A)\) This means: \[ \forall y, \quad y \in C \implies y \in f(A) \] Or equivalently: \[ \forall y \in C, \exists x \in A \text{ such that } f(x) = y \] Two main approaches Approach A (for intervals of real numbers) Pick \(y \in C\) Set \(x\) to be the “inverse” of \(f\) in terms of \(y\) Show \(x \in A\) Plug \(x\) into \(f\) and show \(f(x) = y\) Approach B (for more abstract or multi-variable functions like \(f(x,y)\) ) Pick \(z \in C\) Choose \(x,y\) in terms of \(z\) (sometimes you may need to resort to “fixing” one of the variables to a constant) Show \(f(x,y) = z\) by plugging in and evaluating the function with these new values Essentially, Step 2 is about explicitly constructing a pre-image for every element in \(C\) . Example 1 Claim Let \[ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} \] be defined by \[ f(x) = x^3. \] Then the image of \(f\) is \(\mathbb{R}\) . Meaning, \[ f(\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{R}. \] Proof Step 1: Show \(f(\mathbb{R}) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) Let \(y \in f(\mathbb{R})\) . Then by definition of image, there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ y = f(x) = x^3. \] Since \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) , we have \(x^3 \in \mathbb{R}\) . Thus, \[ y \in \mathbb{R}. \] So, \[ f(\mathbb{R}) \subseteq \mathbb{R}. \] Step 2: Show \(\mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R})\) (Approach A) Let \(y \in \mathbb{R}\) . We must show that there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ f(x) = y. \] Since \[ f(x) = x^3, \] solve for \(x\) in terms of \(y\) : \[ x = \sqrt[3]{y}. \] Since \(y \in \mathbb{R}\) , we have \[ \sqrt[3]{y} \in \mathbb{R}. \] Thus, \[ x \in \mathbb{R}. \] Now compute: \[ f(x) = f(\sqrt[3]{y}) = (\sqrt[3]{y})^3 = y. \] Thus, for every \(y \in \mathbb{R}\) , there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ f(x) = y. \] So, \[ \mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R}). \] Since both inclusions hold, we conclude: \[ f(\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{R}. \] Example 2 Claim Let \[ f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R} \] be defined by \[ f(x,y) = x + y. \] Then the image of \(f\) is \(\mathbb{R}\) . That is, \[ f(\mathbb{R}^2) = \mathbb{R}. \] Proof Step 1: Show \(f(\mathbb{R}^2) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\) Let \[ z \in f(\mathbb{R}^2). \] Then there exists \((x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2\) such that \[ z = f(x,y) = x + y. \] Since \(x,y \in \mathbb{R}\) , it follows that \[ x+y \in \mathbb{R}. \] Thus, \[ z \in \mathbb{R}. \] So, \[ f(\mathbb{R}^2) \subseteq \mathbb{R}. \] Step 2: Show \(\mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R}^2)\) (Approach B) Let \[ z \in \mathbb{R}. \] We must show that there exists \((x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2\) such that \[ f(x,y) = z. \] Construct a pre-image as follows: Let \[ x = z \] and let \[ y = 0. \] Then \[ (x,y) = (z,0) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \] since \(z \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(0 \in \mathbb{R}\) . Now compute: \[ f(z,0) = z + 0 = z. \] Thus, for every \(z \in \mathbb{R}\) , we have constructed an element of \(\mathbb{R}^2\) that maps to it, meaning: \[ \forall z \in \mathbb{R}, \exists (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \text{ such that } f(x,y) = z. \] So, \[ \mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R}^2). \] Since both inclusions hold, we conclude: \[ f(\mathbb{R}^2) = \mathbb{R}. \] Final Note There are certainly more strange image-proof questions you will have to do in this course (I am certain your professors are capable of making a few), but I've tried to cover the most general use-cases in this guide. However, ultimately, keep in mind that mathematics is not a spectator sport. Do more proofs, ask your instructors for feedback if you have that luxury, and find other people to cross-check your work with. Reading these notes and never doing the practice is like watching a 3Blue1Brown video and expecting to become an expert on some mathematics topic. You may come to appreciate the subject more, but you will be far from mastering it without rigorous study. As always, feedback and suggestions in the comments are welcome. I have been busy with university work, so I had this post for a while in my drafts and did not get around to finishing it until now.

How to do an Image/Range Proof

docs.text: If we have a function denoted as \(f\) , defined as \(f: D \to C\) , then \(D\) is the domain, and \(C\) is the codomain. The image of the function is \[ f(D) = \{f(x) \mid x \in D\} \] (the set of all outputs of the function for every input in the domain).
docs.title: Review

docs.text: If we have a function: \[ f: \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \to \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\}, \quad f(x) = x^2 \] We describe the domain and the codomain as the following sets: Domain ( \(D\) ): \[ \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \] Codomain: \[ \{0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5\} \] The range (or image) of the function is: \[ f(D) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in D \} \] Since \[ D = \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}, \] we can evaluate: \[ f(\{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\}) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \} \] \[ = \{ x^2 \mid x \in \{-2, -1, 0, 1, 2\} \} \] \[ = \{(-2)^2, (-1)^2, 0^2, 1^2, 2^2\} \] \[ = \{4, 1, 0\} \] Note: "range" and "image" mean the same thing. You should also notice with this example that even though the image is a subset of the codomain, it may not always be equal to it.
docs.title: Example

Essentially, we want to show: \[ f(A) = C \] To prove two sets are equal, we need double subset inclusion, i.e., prove both: \[ f(A) \subseteq C \] and \[ C \subseteq f(A). \]

docs.text: Let \[ f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R} \] be defined by \[ f(x) = x^3. \] Then the image of \(f\) is \(\mathbb{R}\) . Meaning, \[ f(\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{R}. \]

docs.text: Let \(y \in f(\mathbb{R})\) . Then by definition of image, there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ y = f(x) = x^3. \] Since \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) , we have \(x^3 \in \mathbb{R}\) . Thus, \[ y \in \mathbb{R}. \] So, \[ f(\mathbb{R}) \subseteq \mathbb{R}. \]
docs.title: Step 1: Show \(f(\mathbb{R}) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\)

docs.text: Let \(y \in \mathbb{R}\) . We must show that there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ f(x) = y. \] Since \[ f(x) = x^3, \] solve for \(x\) in terms of \(y\) : \[ x = \sqrt[3]{y}. \] Since \(y \in \mathbb{R}\) , we have \[ \sqrt[3]{y} \in \mathbb{R}. \] Thus, \[ x \in \mathbb{R}. \] Now compute: \[ f(x) = f(\sqrt[3]{y}) = (\sqrt[3]{y})^3 = y. \] Thus, for every \(y \in \mathbb{R}\) , there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ f(x) = y. \] So, \[ \mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R}). \] Since both inclusions hold, we conclude: \[ f(\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{R}. \]
docs.title: Step 2: Show \(\mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R})\) (Approach A)

docs.text: Let \[ f: \mathbb{R}^2 \to \mathbb{R} \] be defined by \[ f(x,y) = x + y. \] Then the image of \(f\) is \(\mathbb{R}\) . That is, \[ f(\mathbb{R}^2) = \mathbb{R}. \]
docs.title: Claim

docs.text: Let \[ z \in f(\mathbb{R}^2). \] Then there exists \((x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2\) such that \[ z = f(x,y) = x + y. \] Since \(x,y \in \mathbb{R}\) , it follows that \[ x+y \in \mathbb{R}. \] Thus, \[ z \in \mathbb{R}. \] So, \[ f(\mathbb{R}^2) \subseteq \mathbb{R}. \]
docs.title: Step 1: Show \(f(\mathbb{R}^2) \subseteq \mathbb{R}\)

docs.text: Let \[ z \in \mathbb{R}. \] We must show that there exists \((x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2\) such that \[ f(x,y) = z. \] Construct a pre-image as follows: Let \[ x = z \] and let \[ y = 0. \] Then \[ (x,y) = (z,0) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \] since \(z \in \mathbb{R}\) and \(0 \in \mathbb{R}\) . Now compute: \[ f(z,0) = z + 0 = z. \] Thus, for every \(z \in \mathbb{R}\) , we have constructed an element of \(\mathbb{R}^2\) that maps to it, meaning: \[ \forall z \in \mathbb{R}, \exists (x,y) \in \mathbb{R}^2 \text{ such that } f(x,y) = z. \] So, \[ \mathbb{R} \subseteq f(\mathbb{R}^2). \] Since both inclusions hold, we conclude: \[ f(\mathbb{R}^2) = \mathbb{R}. \]

docs.title: Final Note

docs.text:
How to Prove a Function is Surjective Those of you who are taking MAT102 currently or in the future may be struggling with how to prove a function is surjective. I've made this guide in an effort to hopefully reduce that anxiety and help you actually understand the concept of a surjective function. Definition of Surjectivity The definition of surjectivity is: \[ 	ext{If we have a function } f: A 	o B, 	ext{ then } B \subseteq f(A). \] By the definition of what it means to be a subset , this means that every element of B is an element of f(A) . Using quantifiers , this can be written as: \[  orall z \, (z \in B \implies z \in f(A)). \] More rigorously, surjectivity means: \[  orall y \in B, \, \exists x \in A 	ext{ such that } f(x) = y. \] General Proof Structure The most common way to prove surjectivity is to use the following proof structure: Let \(y \in B\) . Let \(x =\) "an expression in terms of \(y\) " such that \(x \in A\) (so that \(x\) is a valid input for \(f\) ). In most cases, a good choice is setting \(x\) to the "inverse" of the function. Show that \(f(x) = y\) when plugged in. Example of a Surjectivity Proof Consider the function: \[ 	ext{Let } f: \mathbb{R} 	o [0, \infty), \quad f(x) = x^2. \] We will prove that \(f\) is surjective. Proof Let \[ y \in [0, \infty). \] Since \[ y \geq 0, \] we know that \[ \sqrt{y} \in \mathbb{R} \] (no undefined or imaginary value in the domain/input of \(f\) ), so let \[ x = \sqrt{y}. \] Now compute: \[ f(x) = (\sqrt{y})^2 = y. \] Thus, for all \(y \in [0, \infty)\) , there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ f(x) = y. \] Therefore, \(f\) is surjective. QED. How to Disprove Surjectivity To disprove surjectivity, it is enough to show that there exists an element of B that is not in the image of \(f\) . In quantifiers, this is the negation of the surjectivity statement: \[ 
eg \left(  orall y \in B, \, \exists x \in A 	ext{ such that } f(x) = y ight). \] Which is equivalent to: \[ \exists y \in B 	ext{ such that }  orall x \in A, \, f(x) 
eq y. \] If you can prove this, then \(f\) is not surjective. Intuition Intuitively, disproving surjectivity means: Pick a value in the codomain that the function never outputs. Example of a Non-Surjective Function Consider the function: \[ 	ext{Let } f: \mathbb{Z} 	o \mathbb{Z}, \quad f(x) = 2x. \] We will prove that \(f\) is not surjective. Proof Let \[ y = 1 \] so that \[ y \in \mathbb{Z}. \] For any \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\) , \[ f(x) = 2x \] is always even , so there is no \(x\) such that \[ f(x) = 1 = y, \] since \(1\) is odd . Thus, there exists \(y \in \mathbb{Z}\) such that for all \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\) , \[ f(x) 
eq y. \] Therefore, \(f\) is not surjective. QED. Important Note About the Image of a Function You may realize that \[ f(A) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in A \} \] is just the image (range) of the function by definition. So another way to define surjectivity is to say: \[ f(A) = B. \] Since \[ B \subseteq f(A) \] and \[ f(A) \subseteq B, \] but because the image is always a subset of the codomain of a function, \[ f(A) \subseteq B \] is trivial. So we really only need to prove: \[ B \subseteq f(A). \] Final Thoughts This is just a general approach, and not every question will be solvable by this exact method, but it works for most cases. Regardless, a single algorithm does not exist that works for all surjectivity proofs. Sometimes you will have to use your head more and work with the existing definitions you have. As a wise man once said: "Definition. Definition is always your friend."
docs.title: How to Prove a Function is Surjective

docs.text: Those of you who are taking MAT102 currently or in the future may be struggling with how to prove a function is surjective. I've made this guide in an effort to hopefully reduce that anxiety and help you actually understand the concept of a surjective function.
docs.title: How to Prove a Function is Surjective

docs.text: The definition of surjectivity is: \[ \text{If we have a function } f: A \to B, \text{ then } B \subseteq f(A). \] By the definition of what it means to be a subset , this means that every element of B is an element of f(A) . Using quantifiers , this can be written as: \[ \forall z \, (z \in B \implies z \in f(A)). \] More rigorously, surjectivity means: \[ \forall y \in B, \, \exists x \in A \text{ such that } f(x) = y. \]
docs.title: Definition of Surjectivity

docs.text: The most common way to prove surjectivity is to use the following proof structure: Let \(y \in B\) . Let \(x =\) "an expression in terms of \(y\) " such that \(x \in A\) (so that \(x\) is a valid input for \(f\) ). In most cases, a good choice is setting \(x\) to the "inverse" of the function. Show that \(f(x) = y\) when plugged in.
docs.title: General Proof Structure

docs.text: Consider the function: \[ \text{Let } f: \mathbb{R} \to [0, \infty), \quad f(x) = x^2. \] We will prove that \(f\) is surjective.
docs.title: Example of a Surjectivity Proof

docs.text: Let \[ y \in [0, \infty). \] Since \[ y \geq 0, \] we know that \[ \sqrt{y} \in \mathbb{R} \] (no undefined or imaginary value in the domain/input of \(f\) ), so let \[ x = \sqrt{y}. \] Now compute: \[ f(x) = (\sqrt{y})^2 = y. \] Thus, for all \(y \in [0, \infty)\) , there exists \(x \in \mathbb{R}\) such that \[ f(x) = y. \] Therefore, \(f\) is surjective. QED.
docs.title: Proof

docs.text: To disprove surjectivity, it is enough to show that there exists an element of B that is not in the image of \(f\) . In quantifiers, this is the negation of the surjectivity statement: \[ \neg \left( \forall y \in B, \, \exists x \in A \text{ such that } f(x) = y \right). \] Which is equivalent to: \[ \exists y \in B \text{ such that } \forall x \in A, \, f(x) \neq y. \] If you can prove this, then \(f\) is not surjective.
docs.title: How to Disprove Surjectivity

docs.text: Intuitively, disproving surjectivity means: Pick a value in the codomain that the function never outputs.
docs.title: Intuition

docs.text: Consider the function: \[ \text{Let } f: \mathbb{Z} \to \mathbb{Z}, \quad f(x) = 2x. \] We will prove that \(f\) is not surjective.
docs.title: Example of a Non-Surjective Function

docs.text: Let \[ y = 1 \] so that \[ y \in \mathbb{Z}. \] For any \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\) , \[ f(x) = 2x \] is always even , so there is no \(x\) such that \[ f(x) = 1 = y, \] since \(1\) is odd . Thus, there exists \(y \in \mathbb{Z}\) such that for all \(x \in \mathbb{Z}\) , \[ f(x) \neq y. \] Therefore, \(f\) is not surjective. QED.
docs.title: Proof

docs.text: You may realize that \[ f(A) = \{ f(x) \mid x \in A \} \] is just the image (range) of the function by definition. So another way to define surjectivity is to say: \[ f(A) = B. \] Since \[ B \subseteq f(A) \] and \[ f(A) \subseteq B, \] but because the image is always a subset of the codomain of a function, \[ f(A) \subseteq B \] is trivial. So we really only need to prove: \[ B \subseteq f(A). \]
docs.title: Important Note About the Image of a Function

docs.text: This is just a general approach, and not every question will be solvable by this exact method, but it works for most cases. Regardless, a single algorithm does not exist that works for all surjectivity proofs. Sometimes you will have to use your head more and work with the existing definitions you have. As a wise man once said: "Definition. Definition is always your friend."
docs.title: Final Thoughts
